# Brainstorm: Lichen Sclerosus × ME/CFS — Novel Hypotheses and Research Directions

**Date:** 2026-05-01
**Generated by:** scientific-insight-generator (Opus)
**Status:** Creative analysis — speculative; expert review required before clinical use
**Scope:** Mechanistic hypotheses, research directions, interventions, biomarkers, cross-disease bridges, model extensions

---

## Framing

Lichen sclerosus (LS) and ME/CFS share a Th1-skewed, oxidatively stressed, Treg-dysfunctional immune terrain with overlapping HLA/PTPN22/CTLA4 risk and a common autoimmune comorbidity cluster. No direct epidemiological linkage exists, and the closest proxy (fibromyalgia) showed null association (Halonen 2024). This brainstorm treats the absence of evidence as a research opportunity rather than a refutation: LS is sufficiently rare and ME/CFS sufficiently underdiagnosed that population-scale negative findings are weak. The ideas below are organized by tier (1 = highest plausibility/impact; 3 = long-shot but mechanistically defensible).

A note on certainty: all values are subjective priors, not posterior probabilities from data. Anything ≥ 0.40 has a reasonably traceable mechanistic chain. Anything ≤ 0.20 is intentionally speculative — included because the paper's speculation environment exists for exactly this purpose.

---

## TIER 1 — Highest plausibility/impact

### 1.1 Shared ECM1-axis dysfunction as a connective-tissue-immune bridge

- **Tier:** 1
- **Certainty:** 0.40
- **Mechanism:** ECM1 (extracellular matrix protein 1) regulates basement membrane integrity, binds perlecan and laminin, and modulates angiogenesis. Anti-ECM1 antibodies in LS unmask neoantigens at the dermoepidermal junction. ECM1 is also expressed in vascular endothelium, gut basement membrane, and CNS pericytes. If ME/CFS involves a generalized basement-membrane fragility (consistent with hEDS comorbidity, gut permeability, BBB disruption), ECM1 hypofunction or autoimmunity could be a unifying connective-tissue substrate, with LS as the most clinically visible manifestation.
- **Evidence link:** Oyama 2003 (anti-ECM1 in 75% vulvar LS); ECM1 expression atlas (Human Protein Atlas) shows endothelial and gut expression; ME/CFS BBB and gut barrier findings (Maes 2007, Rodrigues 2024).
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Anti-ECM1 antibodies will be detectable at >2× population baseline prevalence in an unselected ME/CFS cohort (n≥200), and seropositive patients will show elevated zonulin and lower BBB integrity markers (S100B variability) than seronegative ME/CFS controls.
- **Integration target:** Ch07 (Immune Dysfunction) — `hypothesis` environment; cross-reference Ch08 (Neurological/BBB) and Ch11 (Gut barrier).

---

### 1.2 IL-15/IL-7 trans-presentation as the shared cytokine fingerprint

- **Tier:** 1
- **Certainty:** 0.45
- **Mechanism:** Both LS lesions and ME/CFS plasma show elevated IL-15 and IL-7 — homeostatic γc-cytokines that drive memory CD8+ T-cell maintenance and NK survival but also chronic low-grade activation. IL-15 trans-presentation by tissue-resident DCs (skin in LS; possibly muscle/gut in ME/CFS) could sustain a tissue-localized exhausted-effector state without overt systemic inflammation, explaining the "inflamed but not inflammatory" ME/CFS phenotype.
- **Evidence link:** Terlou 2012 (IL-7/IL-15 in LS); Hornig 2015 (early-disease cytokine signature); Mensah 2018 (γc-cytokine review).
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Soluble IL-15Rα in ME/CFS plasma will correlate (r > 0.4) with PEM frequency and inversely with NK cytotoxicity. LS patients with co-existing fatigue will show higher sIL-15Rα than LS-only controls.
- **Integration target:** Ch07 (Immune Dysfunction) — `hypothesis`; biomarker stratification candidate for Ch20.

---

### 1.3 Fibrotic terrain as the irreversibility signal in chronic ME/CFS

- **Tier:** 1
- **Certainty:** 0.40
- **Mechanism:** LS demonstrates that Th1/IFN-γ → TGF-β → fibroblast activation can become self-sustaining and irreversible once established. ME/CFS chronicity (>5y) is poorly explained by current models. Sub-clinical fibrotic remodeling in fascia, perivascular space, or autonomic ganglia — driven by the same TGF-β cascade — could explain why early intervention windows close. LS would be a visible early marker of a patient predisposed to this pathway.
- **Evidence link:** TGF-β1 elevation in long-duration ME/CFS (White 2010, Montoya 2017); fascial stiffness reports (Liptan 2010 — fibromyalgia adjacent); LS fibrosis literature.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Shear-wave elastography of trapezius/thoracolumbar fascia will show higher stiffness in ME/CFS duration > 5y vs < 2y, and stiffness will correlate with serum TGF-β1 and PIIINP (procollagen III N-terminal peptide).
- **Integration target:** Ch13 (Integrative Models) — `hypothesis`; Ch14a (Core Mechanistic) — predisposition terrain.

---

### 1.4 LS as a "trip-switch" prodrome — the immune predisposition hypothesis, sharpened

- **Tier:** 1
- **Certainty:** 0.35
- **Mechanism:** Refines the existing speculation. LS may not cause ME/CFS but identifies women whose Treg/Th1 set-point is already perturbed; a subsequent infectious trigger (EBV, SARS-CoV-2) tips the same terrain into systemic dysregulation. Mechanistically: PTPN22 R620W carriers have both elevated LS risk and elevated post-viral autoimmunity risk; the same lymphocyte signaling threshold underlies both.
- **Evidence link:** PTPN22 R620W in LS (Gao 2005) and post-viral autoimmunity (Bottini 2014); shared HLA-DQ associations.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** In a registry-linkage study, women with biopsy-confirmed LS will have ≥1.5× incidence of ME/CFS following a documented EBV/COVID episode vs matched non-LS controls over 5-year follow-up.
- **Integration target:** Ch05 (Subgroups) — `hypothesis`; Ch04 (Diagnostic framework) — prodromal screening question.

---

## TIER 2 — Mechanistically defensible, evidence sparse

### 2.1 ECM1 autoantibodies cross-react with vascular basement membrane → orthostatic intolerance link

- **Tier:** 2
- **Certainty:** 0.20
- **Mechanism:** ECM1 is expressed in vascular endothelium and pericyte basement membrane. Anti-ECM1 antibodies could weaken venous wall integrity or impair pericyte-endothelial signaling, contributing to venous pooling and small-fiber neuropathy patterns seen in POTS/ME/CFS overlap.
- **Evidence link:** Sercu 2008 (ECM1 vascular expression); POTS small-fiber neuropathy (Novak 2017).
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Anti-ECM1+ ME/CFS patients will have higher prevalence of POTS on tilt-table testing than anti-ECM1− patients (≥2× OR).
- **Integration target:** Ch08 (Autonomic) — `speculation`.

---

### 2.2 Vulvar/genital LS as a sentinel of chronic local hypoxia mirroring systemic ME/CFS hypoperfusion

- **Tier:** 2
- **Certainty:** 0.20
- **Mechanism:** LS lesions show capillary rarefaction and hyaline sclerosis. If ME/CFS patients exhibit microvascular rarefaction systemically (consistent with capillaroscopy abnormalities and reduced VO₂ peak), genital skin — thin, highly vascularized, hormone-sensitive — could be the first tissue to manifest macroscopic damage. LS would then be a "canary" for systemic microcirculatory failure.
- **Evidence link:** ME/CFS capillaroscopy (Newton 2019); LS vascular rarefaction histology; HIF-1α dysregulation reports.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Nailfold capillaroscopy in LS+ME/CFS women will show greater capillary rarefaction than ME/CFS-only women matched for age/duration.
- **Integration target:** Ch09 (Vascular/Microcirculation) — `speculation`.

---

### 2.3 miR-155 / Foxp3 axis as a shared epigenetic switch

- **Tier:** 2
- **Certainty:** 0.30
- **Mechanism:** LS shows elevated miR-155 driving Foxp3 instability and Treg dysfunction. Several ME/CFS miRNA studies (Almenar-Pérez 2020, Petty 2016) flag miR-155 family changes in EVs. A shared miR-155 upregulation could be the convergence point for the shared autoimmune cluster.
- **Evidence link:** Terlou 2012 (LS miR-155); Almenar-Pérez 2020 (ME/CFS miRNA EV cargo).
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Plasma EV miR-155 will be elevated >1.5× in ME/CFS vs controls and will correlate inversely with circulating Treg/Foxp3+ frequency. LS+ME/CFS subgroup will show the highest levels.
- **Integration target:** Ch07 (Immune Dysfunction) — `hypothesis`; Ch20 (Biomarker Research) — candidate panel.

---

### 2.4 Estrogen-receptor crosstalk: post-menopausal LS surge vs. ME/CFS perimenopausal worsening

- **Tier:** 2
- **Certainty:** 0.25
- **Mechanism:** LS peaks post-menopause when estradiol falls; vulvar epithelium loses ERα-mediated trophic and immunomodulatory effects, unmasking Th1 dominance. ME/CFS women frequently report perimenopausal worsening. A shared mechanism: estrogen normally suppresses IFN-γ/IL-15 and supports Treg function; its withdrawal de-represses the Th1 program in both tissues.
- **Evidence link:** Günthert 2008 (estrogen and LS); Thomas 2022 (perimenopause and ME/CFS symptom worsening).
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Topical estradiol responders in LS will show concurrent improvement in fatigue/PEM scores if comorbid ME/CFS is present (pilot n=30).
- **Integration target:** Ch05 (Subgroups — hormonal subgroup) — `hypothesis`; Ch16 (Supplements) flag as research, not recommendation.

---

### 2.5 Tissue-resident memory T-cell (TRM) hypothesis: ME/CFS as a generalized TRM hyperinflammation syndrome

- **Tier:** 2
- **Certainty:** 0.30
- **Mechanism:** LS lesions are dominated by CD8+ TRM cells (CD103+CD69+) that persist locally and re-activate via IL-15. If ME/CFS represents a multi-organ TRM persistence (gut, muscle, brain) following a triggering infection, the systemic phenotype emerges from many tissue-localized TRM niches firing intermittently — explaining symptom waxing/waning and PEM as a TRM reactivation event.
- **Evidence link:** TRM biology (Mackay 2020); LS TRM dominance (Tran 2019); ME/CFS muscle CD8+ infiltrate (Light 2009).
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Muscle biopsy after a PEM episode will show CD103+CD69+ TRM density >2× pre-PEM baseline; blocking IL-15 (anti-IL-15 mAb) will reduce PEM frequency in a small RCT.
- **Integration target:** Ch07 (Immune Dysfunction) — `hypothesis`; Ch14d (Cross-disease) — TRM as a unifying axis.

---

### 2.6 Repurposed clobetasol pulse logic for systemic ME/CFS flares

- **Tier:** 2
- **Certainty:** 0.15 (intervention efficacy); 0.40 (mechanistic basis)
- **Mechanism:** Topical ultra-potent steroids halt LS progression by interrupting the Th1/TGF-β feedback. Brief, low-dose systemic glucocorticoid pulses (NOT chronic) timed to PEM onset have anecdotal ME/CFS use; the LS model justifies a defined pulse protocol rather than chronic suppression. Mechanistic logic: interrupt the IL-15 trans-presentation loop before TRM amplification.
- **Evidence link:** LS clobetasol response (Lee 2015); McKenzie 1998 hydrocortisone trial (mixed); ME/CFS HPA axis literature.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Methylprednisolone 16 mg ×3 days at PEM onset will reduce PEM duration by ≥30% vs placebo in a crossover trial (n=40).
- **Integration target:** Ch18 (Emerging Therapies) — `speculation` framed strictly as research-stage; Ch16 NOT a treatment recommendation. ⚠ chronic steroid use harmful in ME/CFS.

---

### 2.7 Low-dose naltrexone as shared immunomodulator (LS + ME/CFS)

- **Tier:** 2
- **Certainty:** 0.25
- **Mechanism:** LDN reduces TLR4 signaling on microglia and macrophages, attenuates IL-6/TNF, may stabilize Treg function. Both LS and ME/CFS show TLR4-axis activation. LDN already used off-label in ME/CFS with mixed results; LS small case series suggest improvement. A shared trial population would test whether the immune terrain (not the disease label) predicts response.
- **Evidence link:** Younger 2014 (LDN in fibromyalgia); LS LDN case reports (Brown 2021); shared TLR4 mechanism.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** ME/CFS+LS patients will show higher LDN response rate than ME/CFS-only patients (≥1.5× responder ratio at 12 weeks).
- **Integration target:** Ch18 (Emerging Therapies) — `speculation`; flag as off-label.

---

## TIER 3 — Long-shot, intentionally speculative

### 3.1 Chronic occult genital HPV / EBV reactivation as a shared driver

- **Tier:** 3
- **Certainty:** 0.10
- **Mechanism:** LS lesions occasionally show HPV co-detection; some LS cases are associated with chronic Borrelia (in endemic areas). If a low-grade local viral reactivation seeds systemic IFN signatures, the same mechanism could explain post-viral ME/CFS persistence. Speculative chain: persistent local antigen → IFN-γ → systemic Th1 set-point shift.
- **Evidence link:** Borrelia-LS Europe (Eisendle 2008 — disputed); chronic viral reactivation in ME/CFS.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Vulvar swab viral metagenomics in LS+ME/CFS women will show distinct viral signatures vs LS-only controls.
- **Integration target:** Ch12 (Infections/Triggers) — `speculation`; flag low certainty.

---

### 3.2 ECM1 as a mechanosensor: physical deconditioning amplifies ECM1 dysfunction

- **Tier:** 3
- **Certainty:** 0.10
- **Mechanism:** ECM1 expression is mechanically regulated. Prolonged bedrest/deconditioning in ME/CFS may downregulate ECM1 in skin and gut basement membrane, mechanistically worsening barrier integrity over time and creating a feedback loop with rest-induced deterioration (paradoxical effect of pacing-without-loading).
- **Evidence link:** ECM1 mechanoregulation (Sercu 2008); ME/CFS deconditioning controversy.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Skin ECM1 mRNA in long-bedridden ME/CFS patients will be lower than in ambulatory ME/CFS patients matched for severity.
- **Integration target:** Ch17 (Lifestyle) — `speculation` only; do NOT use to justify GET.

---

### 3.3 Hyaluronan fragmentation as a shared DAMP signal

- **Tier:** 3
- **Certainty:** 0.15
- **Mechanism:** LS dermis shows hyaluronan fragmentation; low-MW hyaluronan is a TLR2/4 DAMP driving sterile inflammation. ME/CFS gut and muscle ECM may show similar HA fragmentation, providing a tonic innate immune stimulus. Therapeutic implication: high-MW hyaluronan supplementation or hyaluronidase inhibition.
- **Evidence link:** HA-TLR4 biology (Jiang 2005); LS dermal HA changes.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Serum HA fragment size distribution will be shifted toward LMW in ME/CFS vs controls; correlation with hsCRP and IL-6.
- **Integration target:** Ch07 (Immune Dysfunction) — `speculation`; Ch16 (Supplements) — high-MW HA as research-stage.

---

### 3.4 Topical tacrolimus systemic-axis hypothesis: calcineurin inhibitor pulse for ME/CFS

- **Tier:** 3
- **Certainty:** 0.10
- **Mechanism:** LS responds to topical tacrolimus via local calcineurin inhibition. If ME/CFS involves localized calcineurin/NFAT-driven TRM hyperactivation, a low-dose systemic calcineurin inhibitor pulse (much lower than transplant doses) could be tested. ⚠ HIGH RISK — calcineurin inhibitors have significant toxicity.
- **Evidence link:** Tacrolimus in LS (Goldstein 2011); calcineurin in TRM biology.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Pre-clinical: NFAT activity in ME/CFS PBMCs will be elevated vs controls. If true, justifies cautious dose-finding.
- **Integration target:** Ch18 — `speculation` flagged HIGH RISK; research-only.

---

### 3.5 ECM1 gene therapy / restoration as a unified treatment platform

- **Tier:** 3
- **Certainty:** 0.05
- **Mechanism:** If anti-ECM1 antibodies and ECM1 hypofunction are shared, an ECM1-restoring biologic (recombinant ECM1, ECM1-mimetic peptide, or autoantibody-removal via immunoadsorption) could address both LS lesions and the systemic basement-membrane fragility in a subset of ME/CFS.
- **Evidence link:** ECM1 knockout mouse phenotype (Hamada 2003 — lipoid proteinosis); immunoadsorption efficacy in autoimmune disease.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Immunoadsorption of anti-ECM1 antibodies in seropositive ME/CFS patients will improve fatigue scores by ≥20% at 12 weeks vs sham.
- **Integration target:** Ch18 (Emerging Therapies) — `speculation`, frame as decade-out concept.

---

### 3.6 Quercetin + N-acetylcysteine as shared oxidative-stress + Th1-modulation stack

- **Tier:** 3
- **Certainty:** 0.20 (mechanism); 0.10 (clinical efficacy)
- **Mechanism:** Both LS and ME/CFS show elevated 8-OHdG/MDA and reduced GSH. Quercetin attenuates IFN-γ-driven Th1 polarization in vitro; NAC restores glutathione and reduces TLR4-driven inflammation. The combination targets the shared oxidative-Th1 terrain.
- **Evidence link:** Quercetin Th1 modulation (Li 2016); NAC in fatigue (Arranz 2007); LS oxidative stress (Sander 2004).
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Open-label quercetin 500 mg + NAC 1200 mg/day for 12 weeks will reduce serum 8-OHdG by ≥25% in LS+ME/CFS; correlation with PEM frequency reduction.
- **Integration target:** Ch16 (Supplements) — `speculation` framed as research-stage; flag interactions and explicitly NOT a clinical recommendation per Hypothesis-vs-Recommendation Distinction.

---

### 3.7 Vitamin D-VDR axis as a shared regulator

- **Tier:** 3
- **Certainty:** 0.15
- **Mechanism:** Vitamin D deficiency is more common in LS and is hypothesized in ME/CFS. VDR activation suppresses IFN-γ and supports Treg/Foxp3 stability — exactly the axis disturbed in both. Shared VDR polymorphism (FokI, BsmI) studies could test convergent risk.
- **Evidence link:** Kreuter 2014 (vitamin D in LS); Berkovitz 2009 (vitamin D and ME/CFS).
- **Falsifiable prediction:** VDR FokI ff genotype frequency will be higher in LS+ME/CFS than LS-only or ME/CFS-only.
- **Integration target:** Ch16 (Supplements) — already standard nutritional optimization; Ch20 (Biomarkers) — VDR genotyping as stratifier.

---

## Cross-disease bridges (Category 6)

### B1. Morphea / systemic sclerosis as the closer ME/CFS comparator

- **Tier:** 2 / Certainty: 0.30
- Morphea (localized scleroderma) shares LS's IFN-γ→TGF-β fibrotic mechanism but more often co-occurs with fatigue syndromes. SSc has documented severe fatigue (often meeting ME/CFS criteria). Studying SSc-fatigue at the molecular level (IFN signature, TRM density) could yield ME/CFS-relevant findings faster than direct LS studies.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** SSc patients with high IFN signature scores will show higher rates of ME/CFS-criteria-meeting fatigue than low-IFN-signature SSc patients.
- **Integration target:** Ch14d (Cross-disease) — `hypothesis`.

### B2. Vitiligo as a non-fibrotic Th1-CD8-TRM comparator

- **Tier:** 2 / Certainty: 0.25
- Vitiligo shares CD8 TRM + IFN-γ + IL-15 trans-presentation with LS but without fibrosis. If vitiligo+ME/CFS patients exist as a distinct subgroup, they would test whether the fibrotic axis or the TRM axis is the ME/CFS-relevant one.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Vitiligo prevalence in ME/CFS will be elevated similarly to or more than other autoimmune comorbidities.
- **Integration target:** Ch14d (Cross-disease) — `hypothesis`.

### B3. Lipoid proteinosis (ECM1 loss-of-function) as a natural experiment

- **Tier:** 3 / Certainty: 0.15
- Patients with biallelic ECM1 loss-of-function (lipoid proteinosis) provide a natural experiment for systemic ECM1 deficiency. Case-finding fatigue prevalence in this rare cohort would inform the ECM1-ME/CFS link.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Lipoid proteinosis registries will show elevated fatigue/PEM-like symptom prevalence vs general population.
- **Integration target:** Appendix H (Annotated Bibliography) flag as research direction; Ch20.

---

## Mathematical / model extensions (Category 7)

### M1. Add tissue-resident memory T-cell compartment to ME/CFS dynamic models

- **Tier:** 2 / Certainty: 0.35
- Current ME/CFS state-space models (energy/HPA/immune) lack a TRM compartment. LS provides quantitative TRM dynamics: density, IL-15-dependent persistence, reactivation thresholds. Adding a TRM state variable with IL-15-driven amplification would predict PEM dynamics differently from current models (delayed peak, threshold-based reactivation).
- **Falsifiable prediction:** A TRM-augmented model will fit observed PEM time-courses (delayed onset, prolonged decay) with lower error than non-TRM models.
- **Integration target:** Ch13 (Integrative Models) — formal extension; or formalization volume.

### M2. Add basement-membrane integrity parameter (ECM1-dependent)

- **Tier:** 3 / Certainty: 0.20
- Introduce a barrier-integrity scalar β ∈ [0,1] modulated by anti-ECM1 antibody titer. Couple β to gut translocation rate and BBB permeability terms. Predicts that anti-ECM1+ patients have non-linear amplification of LPS-driven inflammation.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** In anti-ECM1+ ME/CFS, LBP/LPS-binding protein will scale super-linearly with disease severity vs anti-ECM1−.
- **Integration target:** Ch13 / formalization volume.

### M3. Estrogen-modulated Treg setpoint as a time-varying parameter

- **Tier:** 2 / Certainty: 0.25
- Add E2(t)-dependent term to Treg dynamics; predicts perimenopausal worsening and post-menopausal LS emergence as bifurcation events in the same model.
- **Falsifiable prediction:** Model fit to longitudinal symptom data will require time-varying Treg parameter for women, not for men.
- **Integration target:** Ch13 — `hypothesis`; Ch05 subgroups.

## Diagnostic / biomarker ideas (Category 5) — consolidated

| ID | Marker | Use | Tier | Certainty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | Anti-ECM1 antibody panel | Stratification: identify autoimmune-terrain ME/CFS subgroup | 1 | 0.40 |
| D2 | Soluble IL-15Rα | Trans-presentation activity proxy; PEM correlate | 1 | 0.35 |
| D3 | EV miR-155 | Treg dysfunction proxy | 2 | 0.30 |
| D4 | LMW hyaluronan fraction | DAMP burden | 3 | 0.15 |
| D5 | Skin biopsy CD103+CD69+ TRM density | Tissue TRM burden index (research only) | 2 | 0.30 |
| D6 | VDR FokI genotype | Predisposition stratifier | 3 | 0.15 |
| D7 | Vulvar exam at ME/CFS intake (women) | Cheap clinical screen for LS comorbidity | 1 | 0.50 (feasibility) |

---

## Research directions (Category 2) — prioritized

| # | Study | Design | Feasibility | Tier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | LS prevalence in ME/CFS women | Cross-sectional, gynecologic exam, n≥300 | High | 1 |
| R2 | ME/CFS incidence post-LS diagnosis | Registry linkage (Nordic registries best) | Medium | 1 |
| R3 | Anti-ECM1 antibody screen in ME/CFS | Plasma assay, n≥200 | High | 1 |
| R4 | Shear-wave elastography of fascia by duration | Cross-sectional imaging | Medium | 1 |
| R5 | Vulvar+nailfold capillaroscopy comparison | Case-control | Medium | 2 |
| R6 | LDN responder rate by autoimmune comorbidity | RCT secondary analysis | High | 2 |
| R7 | Methylpred pulse at PEM onset | Crossover RCT, n=40 | Medium | 2 |
| R8 | TRM density in muscle biopsy pre/post PEM | Invasive — ethically demanding | Low | 2 |
| R9 | Quercetin+NAC open-label oxidative markers | Single-arm trial, n=30 | High | 3 |
| R10 | Lipoid proteinosis fatigue survey | Registry-based questionnaire | High (small N) | 3 |

## Risk assessment summary

| Intervention | Risk | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Vulvar exam screening | ✓ Low | Standard gynecologic care |
| Quercetin / NAC | ✓ Low | OTC; check interactions |
| LDN | ✓ Low–medium | Off-label, monitored |
| Topical tacrolimus | ✓ Low (local) | Established LS use |
| Methylprednisolone pulse | ⚠ Medium | Time-limited only; chronic use harmful |
| Anti-IL-15 mAb | ⚠ High | Trial-stage; immunosuppressive |
| Calcineurin inhibitors | ⚠ High | Significant toxicity profile |
| Immunoadsorption | ⚠ High | Procedural risk |

---

## Caveats

- All certainty values are subjective priors. Treat as ordinal, not Bayesian.
- The strongest single null finding (Halonen 2024 fibromyalgia-LS) is a meaningful constraint; ideas above must survive a future direct ME/CFS-LS epidemiological study to retain plausibility.
- Treatment ideas in Tier 2/3 are mechanistic rationales, NOT clinical recommendations. Per project convention, these must be flagged with `warning` or `limitation` environments in any treatment chapter integration.
- The ECM1 axis (1.1, 2.1, 3.5, M2) is the strongest connective thread across this brainstorm; if it fails to replicate (anti-ECM1 antibodies absent or unrelated to ME/CFS phenotype), several Tier 1–2 ideas collapse together.
- Falsifiability: every idea above has at least one falsifiable prediction. Per A11 audit convention, this is a hard requirement.

---

## Suggested next steps for the user

1. **Cheap, high-yield:** add gynecologic-history question ("Have you been diagnosed with vulvar lichen sclerosus or chronic vulvar itching/whitening?") to ME/CFS intake questionnaires. Could be done immediately by clinician collaborators.
2. **Tier 1 integration:** ideas 1.1–1.4 are ready for `hypothesis` environment integration in Ch05/Ch07/Ch13 with explicit certainty labels.
3. **Research collaboration:** R1 and R3 are feasible with existing ME/CFS biobanks (UK ME/CFS Biobank, NIH ME/CFS Collaborative).
4. **Expert review needed:** dermatologist + immunologist + ME/CFS clinician triad before any treatment-related integration (ideas 2.6, 2.7, 3.4, 3.6).
